communityVoices.quote: Eyes is very jazzy and upbeat with crisp keys providing the perfect background for Jerry to play to. The keys are so prominent it's almost approaching ragtime.
communityVoices.attribution: enjoy every (dead) sandwich, on Eyes of the World

communityVoices.quote: Phil had his guitar wired each string to four huge stacks of speakers about 70 yards from the stage. He played with the stadium echo during Playing in the Band and I haven't been the same since.
communityVoices.attribution: Sweetmd3, on Playing in the Band

communityVoices.quote: Super 73' Soundboard! 5 stars for the mix, recording, performance & transfer.
i feel safe secured and whole for my land is a national park
SHARE...SHARE...SHARE....that's what this is about...it's not about your hate for someone that is helping people enjoy life.

whyThisShow: This is a classic 1973 wall-of-sound show at an enormous outdoor venue, with a soundboard recording that one listener rated five stars across "the mix, recording, performance & transfer" [source: 'Super 73\' Soundboard! 5 stars for the mix, recording, performance & transfer']. One reviewer called it "a great representation of this year" and noted the band's ability to "transition so fluidly from rock riffs to jazz grooves to spacey, surrealist jams" [source: 'a great representation of this year']. The Eyes of the World drew particular attention — one listener found it "very jazzy and upbeat with crisp keys providing the perfect background for Jerry to play to" and noted the keys were "so prominent it's almost approaching ragtime" [source: 'Eyes is very jazzy and upbeat with crisp keys providing the perfect background for Jerry to play to. The keys are so prominent it\'s almost approaching ragtime']. The show is unreleased officially, which makes this clean soundboard the community's primary window into the night.
listenFor: Phil's bass work is a recurring reference point throughout the reviews — one listener described how "Phil's bass slaps me in the face at the 10 minute mark" in He's Gone [source: 'Phil\'s bass slaps me in the face at the 10 minute mark in this song']. The Playing in the Band carries extra weight given the venue: one attendee recalled that Phil "played with the stadium echo during Playing in the Band" using speakers stacked "about 70 yards from the stage" [source: 'Phil had his guitar wired each string to four huge stacks of speakers about 70 yards from the stage. He played with the stadium echo during Playing in the Band']. The closing NFA > Goin' Down the Road > NFA sequence earned specific praise as something to savor [source: 'the NFA>GDTRFB>NFA from this show is certainly a sandwich to be enjoyed']. One reviewer also flagged an unexplained recurring sound — "sounds almost like a slapstick or even fireworks" — that pops up throughout the recording [source: 'sounds almost like a slapstick or even fireworks'].
context: June 10, 1973 was the second of two consecutive Dead shows at RFK Stadium in Washington, DC — the Archive also holds June 9 from the same run. This is the wall-of-sound era, when Phil Lesh's bass rig was famously wired to massive speaker stacks positioned deep in the venue. The recording is a soundboard (identifier: gd73-06-10.sbd.hollister.174.sbeok.shnf) and has never received an official release, making the Archive tape the community's standard source. One reviewer noted that the Allman Brothers were at the show and joined for a third set, calling that portion "a classic" [source: 'The whole third set with the Allman Bros is a classic in my opinion'].
funFact: One attendee recalled that after Deadheads entered RFK, "some of them opened the other gates" so the venue ran out of food — and when he and his future wife tried to leave, a rent-a-cop on a scooter spooked them so badly they "thought that they had decided to keep us all here for observation or something" [source: 'some of them opened the other gates'].
headline: RFK in the summer heat, Phil wired to the sky.
